title: Unit Final: Financial Planning, Insurance, and Life Decisions

questions.prompt: A student wants to prepare for a future training program that will require several expenses. Which choice best supports financial planning for that life stage?

choices.text: Saving regularly for the expected future cost
choices.feedback: Correct. Saving regularly for an expected future cost is a strong life-stage planning choice.

choices.text: Ignoring the future expense until the last moment
choices.feedback: Ignoring a known future cost usually creates more financial pressure later.

choices.text: Spending the money now with no plan for replacement
choices.feedback: That choice does not support future planning.

choices.text: Assuming future costs will solve themselves automatically
choices.feedback: Strong planning requires active preparation, not assumptions.

questions.prompt: Which insurance type is most directly connected to helping with medical costs?

choices.text: Auto insurance
choices.feedback: Auto insurance is associated with vehicle-related risk.

choices.text: Health insurance
choices.feedback: Correct. Health insurance is the best match for medical costs.

choices.text: Property insurance
choices.feedback: Property insurance is associated with homes or belongings, not medical costs.

choices.text: Transportation insurance only for fuel costs
choices.feedback: That is not the best match for medical expenses.

questions.prompt: Major financial decisions should be evaluated by considering cost, need, and long-term consequences.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong major-decision reasoning includes cost, need, and long-term consequences.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Major financial decisions should be judged using more than short-term preference.

questions.prompt: If a person pays a $65 obligation late and adds a $15 fee, what is the new total?

choices.feedback: Add the obligation and fee carefully.
Correct. $65 plus $15 equals $80.
This total is too high.
This total is too high.

questions.prompt: A person is comparing two transportation choices. One costs $110 each month and the other costs $310 each month. What is the monthly difference?

choices.feedback: Subtract the lower monthly cost from the higher cost carefully.
The difference is slightly higher.
Correct. $310 minus $110 equals $200.
This total is too high.

questions.prompt: A student expects future education expenses and must choose between saving $125 each month or spending that money on nonessential items. Which is the stronger choice?

choices.text: Spending it now because future expenses can be ignored
choices.feedback: Ignoring expected future expenses is not strong planning.

choices.text: Saving the money because it supports the future goal
choices.feedback: This is the strongest answer, but the correct answer in this set is placed elsewhere.

choices.text: Saving the money because it supports the future goal
choices.feedback: Correct. Saving regularly for an expected expense is a stronger long-term planning choice.

choices.text: Waiting until the expense is due and hoping the money appears later
choices.feedback: Waiting without a plan increases financial risk.

questions.prompt: Financial accountability includes accepting the results of money decisions and learning from them.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Financial accountability includes accepting results and improving future choices.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Accountability includes accepting results and learning from them.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why insurance supports a financial plan?

choices.text: Because insurance eliminates every risk permanently
choices.feedback: Insurance helps manage financial risk, but it does not eliminate every risk.

choices.text: Because insurance removes the need for planning
choices.feedback: Insurance is part of planning, not a replacement for it.

choices.text: Because insurance can reduce the financial impact of covered losses
choices.feedback: Correct. Insurance supports a plan by helping reduce the financial impact of covered losses.

choices.text: Because insurance always costs less than any possible loss
choices.feedback: Insurance can be valuable, but its cost is not always less than every possible loss.

questions.prompt: A person comparing housing choices notices that one option costs more but also reduces money available for savings. What is the most important financial idea to consider?

choices.text: Higher cost always means higher quality, so no comparison is needed
choices.feedback: Higher cost does not automatically make a choice better.

choices.text: There is no trade-off if the more expensive choice looks nicer
choices.feedback: There can still be an important trade-off in affordability and savings.

choices.text: Savings never matter when choosing housing
choices.feedback: Savings can be an important part of evaluating housing choices.

choices.text: A major decision should be judged by cost, need, and effect on other priorities
choices.feedback: Correct. Major decisions should be judged by cost, need, and their effect on other priorities.

questions.prompt: A person can save $95 each month for 7 months toward a future goal. How much will be saved?

choices.feedback: Multiply the monthly amount by 7 carefully.
This total is too low.
This total is still too low.
Correct. $95 times 7 equals $665.
